content.content:
1	/**
2	 * The Animator — public types.
3	 *
4	 * These types form the contract between The Animator apparatus and all
5	 * callers (summon relay, nsg consult, etc.). No implementation details.
6	 *
7	 * See: docs/specification.md (animator)
8	 */
9	
10	import type { AnimaWeave } from '@shardworks/loom-apparatus';
11	import type { ResolvedTool } from '@shardworks/tools-apparatus';
12	
13	// ── Session chunks (streaming output) ────────────────────────────────
14	
15	/** A chunk of output from a running session. */
16	export type SessionChunk =
17	  | { type: 'text'; text: string }
18	  | { type: 'tool_use'; tool: string }
19	  | { type: 'tool_result'; tool: string };
20	
21	// ── Request / Result ─────────────────────────────────────────────────
22	
23	export interface AnimateRequest {
24	  /**
25	   * Optional pre-generated session id. When provided, the Animator uses
26	   * this id instead of generating a new one. Used by summon() to make the
27	   * session id available on the handle before the Loom weave resolves.
28	   */
29	  sessionId?: string;
30	  /** The anima weave from The Loom (composed identity context). */
31	  context: AnimaWeave;
32	  /**
33	   * The work prompt — what the anima should do.
34	   * Passed directly to the session provider as the initial prompt.
35	   * This bypasses The Loom — it is not a composition concern.
36	   */
37	  prompt?: string;
38	  /**
39	   * Working directory for the session.
40	   * The session provider launches the AI process here.
41	   */
42	  cwd: string;
43	  /**
44	   * Optional conversation id to resume a multi-turn conversation.
45	   * If provided, the session provider resumes the existing conversation
46	   * rather than starting a new one.
47	   */
48	  conversationId?: string;
49	  /**
50	   * Caller-supplied metadata recorded alongside the session.
51	   * The Animator stores this as-is — it does not interpret the contents.
52	   */
53	  metadata?: Record<string, unknown>;
54	  /**
55	   * Enable streaming output. When true, the returned `chunks` iterable
56	   * yields output as the session produces it. When false (default), the
57	   * `chunks` iterable completes immediately with no items.
58	   *
59	   * Either way, the return shape is the same: `{ chunks, result }`.
60	   */
61	  streaming?: boolean;
62	  /**
63	   * Task-layer environment variables. Overrides the identity-layer
64	   * environment from the AnimaWeave when keys collide. Spread into the
65	   * spawned process environment.
66	   */
67	  environment?: Record<string, string>;
68	}
69	
70	export interface SessionResult {
71	  /** Unique session id (generated by The Animator). */
72	  id: string;
73	  /**
74	   * Terminal status.
75	   *
76	   * `'rate-limited'` signals that the session terminated because the
77	   * underlying anima provider reported (or was detected to be) rate
78	   * limited. Callers that dispatched through The Animator see this as
79	   * a first-class terminal outcome distinct from `'failed'`, so that
80	   * the Animator's back-off gate can govern the next dispatch and
81	   * engines/rigs can route to the reversible-wait path rather than
82	   * treating the session as a generic failure.
83	   */
84	  status: 'completed' | 'failed' | 'timeout' | 'cancelled' | 'rate-limited';
85	  /** When the session started (ISO-8601). */
86	  startedAt: string;
87	  /** When the session ended (ISO-8601). */
88	  endedAt: string;
89	  /** Wall-clock duration in milliseconds. */
90	  durationMs: number;
91	  /** Provider name (e.g. 'claude-code'). */
92	  provider: string;
93	  /** Numeric exit code from the provider process. */
94	  exitCode: number;
95	  /** Error message if failed. */
96	  error?: string;
97	  /** Conversation id (for multi-turn resume). */
98	  conversationId?: string;
99	  /** Session id from the provider (e.g. for --resume). */
100	  providerSessionId?: string;
101	  /** Token usage from the provider, if available. */
102	  tokenUsage?: TokenUsage;
103	  /** Cost in USD from the provider, if available. */
104	  costUsd?: number;
105	  /** Caller-supplied metadata, recorded as-is. */
106	  metadata?: Record<string, unknown>;
107	  /**
108	   * The final assistant text from the session.
109	   * Extracted by the Animator from the provider's transcript.
110	   * Useful for programmatic consumers that need the session's conclusion
111	   * without parsing the full transcript (e.g. the Spider's review collect step).
112	   */
113	  output?: string;
114	  /**
115	   * Structured termination tag attached by the session provider when the
116	   * terminal status reflects a specific detected condition (today:
117	   * rate-limit detection). Absent when the terminal status has no extra
118	   * structure. Consumers that just need the outcome should branch on
119	   * `status`; the Animator's back-off machine uses this tag to
120	   * disambiguate rate-limit terminations from generic failures.
121	   */
122	  terminationTag?: SessionTerminationTag;
123	}
124	
125	export interface TokenUsage {
126	  inputTokens: number;
127	  outputTokens: number;
128	  cacheReadTokens?: number;
129	  cacheWriteTokens?: number;
130	}
131	
132	// ── Bulk session cost lookup ─────────────────────────────────────────
133	
134	/**
135	 * Per-session cost snapshot returned by AnimatorApi.getSessionCosts().
136	 *
137	 * A projection of the cost/token fields on SessionDoc — sufficient for
138	 * bulk cost aggregation (rig-level totals, per-engine breakdowns) without
139	 * forcing the caller to reach into the sessions book directly. Consumers
140	 * that need other session fields should look them up via SessionDoc.
141	 */
142	export interface SessionCost {
143	  /**
144	   * Cost in USD as reported by the session. Zero when the session exists
145	   * but has not reported a cost (e.g. still running, or the provider did
146	   * not record one).
147	   */
148	  costUsd: number;
149	  /** Input tokens from the session's tokenUsage, if the provider reported it. */
150	  inputTokens?: number;
151	  /** Output tokens from the session's tokenUsage, if the provider reported it. */
152	  outputTokens?: number;
153	}
154	
155	// ── Summon request ──────────────────────────────────────────────────
156	
157	export interface SummonRequest {
158	  /**
159	   * The work prompt — what the anima should do.
160	   * Passed directly to the session provider as the initial prompt.
161	   */
162	  prompt: string;
163	  /**
164	   * The role to summon (e.g. 'artificer', 'scribe').
165	   * Passed to The Loom for context composition and recorded in session metadata.
166	   */
167	  role?: string;
168	  /**
169	   * Working directory for the session.
170	   * The session provider launches the AI process here.
171	   */
172	  cwd: string;
173	  /**
174	   * Optional conversation id to resume a multi-turn conversation.
175	   */
176	  conversationId?: string;
177	  /**
178	   * Additional metadata to record alongside the session.
179	   * Merged with auto-generated metadata (trigger: 'summon', role).
180	   */
181	  metadata?: Record<string, unknown>;
182	  /**
183	   * Enable streaming output. When true, the returned `chunks` iterable
184	   * yields output as the session produces it. When false (default), the
185	   * `chunks` iterable completes immediately with no items.
186	   */
187	  streaming?: boolean;
188	  /**
189	   * Task-layer environment variables. Overrides the identity-layer
190	   * environment from the AnimaWeave when keys collide. Spread into the
191	   * spawned process environment.
192	   */
193	  environment?: Record<string, string>;
194	}
195	
196	// ── Animator API (the `provides` interface) ──────────────────────────
197	
198	/** The return value from animate() and summon(). */
199	export interface AnimateHandle {
200	  /**
201	   * Session ID, available immediately after launch — before the session
202	   * completes. Callers that only need to know the session was launched
203	   * (e.g. quick engines returning `{ status: 'launched', sessionId }`)
204	   * can return without awaiting `result`.
205	   */
206	  sessionId: string;
207	  /**
208	   * Async iterable of output chunks from the session. When streaming is
209	   * disabled (the default), this iterable completes immediately with no
210	   * items. When streaming is enabled, it yields chunks as the session
211	   * produces output.
212	   */
213	  chunks: AsyncIterable<SessionChunk>;
214	  /**
215	   * Promise that resolves to the final SessionResult after the session
216	   * completes (or fails/times out) and the result is recorded to The Stacks.
217	   */
218	  result: Promise<SessionResult>;
219	}
220	
221	export interface AnimatorApi {
222	  /**
223	   * Summon an anima — compose context via The Loom and launch a session.
224	   *
225	   * This is the high-level "make an anima do a thing" entry point.
226	   * Internally calls The Loom for context composition (passing the role),
227	   * then animate() for session launch and recording. The work prompt
228	   * bypasses the Loom and goes directly to the provider.
229	   *
230	   * Requires The Loom apparatus to be installed. Throws if not available.
231	   *
232	   * Auto-populates session metadata with `trigger: 'summon'` and `role`.
233	   *
234	   * Returns synchronously — the async work lives inside `result` and `chunks`.
235	   */
236	  summon(request: SummonRequest): AnimateHandle;
237	
238	  /**
239	   * Animate a session — launch an AI process with the given context.
240	   *
241	   * This is the low-level entry point for callers that compose their own
242	   * AnimaWeave (e.g. The Parlour for multi-turn conversations).
243	   *
244	   * Records the session result to The Stacks before `result` resolves.
245	   *
246	   * Set `streaming: true` on the request to receive output chunks as the
247	   * session runs. When streaming is disabled (default), the `chunks`
248	   * iterable completes immediately with no items.
249	   *
250	   * Returns synchronously — the async work lives inside `result` and `chunks`.
251	   */
252	  animate(request: AnimateRequest): AnimateHandle;
253	
254	  /**
255	   * Subscribe to a running session's output stream.
256	   *
257	   * Returns an AsyncIterable that replays all chunks already produced
258	   * (from session start) and then yields new chunks as they arrive.
259	   * Returns null if there is no active in-memory session with the given id
260	   * (i.e. the session is unknown, already completed, or started before this
261	   * process started).
262	   *
263	   * When the session ends, the iterable completes automatically.
264	   */
265	  subscribeToSession(sessionId: string): AsyncIterable<SessionChunk> | null;
266	
267	  /**
268	   * Cancel a running session.
269	   *
270	   * Patches the SessionDoc to 'cancelled' with endedAt, durationMs, and
271	   * the optional reason in the error field. If cancelHandle is available,
272	   * delegates to the provider's cancel() method to kill the process.
273	   *
274	   * Idempotent: if the session is already in a terminal state, returns the
275	   * existing SessionDoc without modification. Throws if the session ID
276	   * does not exist.
277	   *
278	   * Does NOT wait for the process to die — returns immediately after
279	   * patching and sending the kill signal.
280	   */
281	  cancel(sessionId: string, options?: { reason?: string }): Promise<SessionDoc>;
282	
283	  /**
284	   * Bulk per-session cost/token lookup — the first read-side helper on
285	   * AnimatorApi. Resolves cost and token-usage snapshots for the given
286	   * session ids in a single round-trip against the sessions book.
287	   *
288	   * Returns a Map keyed by session id. Session ids not present in the
289	   * sessions book are omitted from the returned Map — callers decide
290	   * whether that means "zero contribution" (as Spider's rig-view does) or
291	   * something else.
292	   *
293	   * Empty input returns an empty Map without touching The Stacks.
294	   *
295	   * Intended for UI-facing aggregators that compose rig-level totals or
296	   * per-engine breakdowns. The shape is deliberately minimal: callers that
297	   * need other SessionDoc fields should look them up separately.
298	   */
299	  getSessionCosts(sessionIds: string[]): Promise<Map<string, SessionCost>>;
300	
301	  /**
302	   * Return the current Animator status document verbatim.
303	   *
304	   * Exposes the back-off state machine's persisted state — pause phase,
305	   * window bounds, back-off level, and triggering session — to callers
306	   * (Spider's crawl gate, the `animator-status` CLI tool, the
307	   * `GET /api/animator/status` HTTP route, the Oculus banner). The
308	   * dispatchability decision is delegated to the canonical
309	   * `isDispatchable(doc)` helper exported from this package; consumers
310	   * call that helper rather than re-composing the predicate themselves.
311	   * The HTTP route additionally enriches the response with a
312	   * server-computed `dispatchable: boolean` field for non-TS consumers
313	   * — see the `animator-status` tool.
314	   *
315	   * Returns an AnimatorStatusDoc with `state: 'running'` and
316	   * `backoffLevel: 0` on fresh installs that have never hit a rate
317	   * limit — the shape is always defined so callers don't need a null
318	   * check.
319	   */
320	  getStatus(): Promise<AnimatorStatusDoc>;
321	}
322	
323	// ── Session provider interface ───────────────────────────────────────
324	
325	/**
326	 * A session provider — pluggable backend that knows how to launch and
327	 * communicate with a specific AI system.
328	 *
329	 * Implemented as an apparatus plugin whose `provides` object satisfies
330	 * this interface. The Animator discovers the provider via guild config:
331	 * `guild.json["animator"]["sessionProvider"]` names the plugin id.
332	 *
333	 * The provider always returns `{ chunks, result }` — the same shape as
334	 * AnimateHandle. When `config.streaming` is true, the provider MAY yield
335	 * output chunks as the session runs. When false (or when the provider
336	 * does not support streaming), the chunks iterable completes immediately
337	 * with no items. The Animator does not branch on streaming capability —
338	 * it passes the flag through and trusts the provider to do the right thing.
339	 */
340	export interface AnimatorSessionProvider {
341	  /** Human-readable name (e.g. 'claude-code'). */
342	  name: string;
343	
344	  /**
345	   * Launch a session. Returns `{ chunks, result }` synchronously.
346	   *
347	   * The `result` promise resolves when the AI process exits.
348	   * The `chunks` async iterable yields output when `config.streaming`
349	   * is true and the provider supports streaming; otherwise it completes
350	   * immediately with no items.
351	   *
352	   * Providers that don't support streaming simply ignore the flag and
353	   * return empty chunks — no separate method needed.
354	   */
355	  launch(config: SessionProviderConfig): {
356	    chunks: AsyncIterable<SessionChunk>;
357	    result: Promise<SessionProviderResult>;
358	    /**
359	     * Provider-specific process metadata for cross-process cancellation.
360	     * Resolves as soon as the process is spawned. The Animator persists the
361	     * resolved value as `cancelHandle` on the SessionDoc.
362	     *
363	     * Optional — providers that don't support cancellation omit this.
364	     */
365	    processInfo?: Promise<CancelHandle>;
366	  };
367	
368	  /**
369	   * Cancel a running session using previously persisted process metadata.
370	   *
371	   * Called by the Animator with the cancelMetadata from the SessionDoc.
372	   * The provider interprets the metadata and kills the process.
373	   *
374	   * Optional — providers that don't support cancellation omit this.
375	   */
376	  cancel?(cancelMetadata: CancelHandle): Promise<void>;
377	}
378	
379	export interface SessionProviderConfig {
380	  /**
381	   * Pre-generated session id. The Animator generates this before calling
382	   * launch() so both sides share the same id. Providers that spawn detached
383	   * processes need this to correlate lifecycle events (session-running,
384	   * session-record) back to the Animator's session record.
385	   */
386	  sessionId: string;
387	  /** System prompt for the AI process. May be undefined if composition is not yet implemented. */
388	  systemPrompt?: string;
389	  /** Initial user message (e.g. writ description). */
390	  initialPrompt?: string;
391	  /** Model to use (from guild settings). */
392	  model: string;
393	  /** Optional conversation id for resume. */
394	  conversationId?: string;
395	  /** Working directory for the session. */
396	  cwd: string;
397	  /**
398	   * Enable streaming output. When true, the provider should yield output
399	   * chunks as the session produces them. When false (default), the chunks
400	   * iterable should complete immediately with no items.
401	   *
402	   * Providers that don't support streaming may ignore this flag.
403	   */
404	  streaming?: boolean;
405	  /**
406	   * Resolved tools for this session. When present, the provider should
407	   * configure an MCP server with these tool definitions.
408	   *
409	   * The Loom resolves role → permissions → tools via the Instrumentarium.
410	   * The Animator passes them through from the AnimaWeave.
411	   */
412	  tools?: ResolvedTool[];
413	  /**
414	   * Merged environment variables to spread into the spawned process.
415	   * The Animator merges identity-layer (weave) and task-layer (request)
416	   * variables before passing them here — task layer wins on collision.
417	   */
418	  environment?: Record<string, string>;
419	}
420	
421	/** A single message from the NDJSON stream. Untyped — shape varies by provider. */
422	export type TranscriptMessage = Record<string, unknown>;
423	
424	/**
425	 * Structured termination tag attached by a session provider when it
426	 * detects a specific non-generic reason for a session's terminal state.
427	 *
428	 * Today the only kind is `'rate-limit'` — the claude-code provider raises
429	 * this when it observes a rate-limit signature in a structured NDJSON
430	 * message (either the message `subtype` or a structured `is_error`
431	 * payload). The Animator consumes the tag to drive its back-off state
432	 * machine; consumers that only care about the outcome can ignore the
433	 * tag and branch on status instead.
434	 *
435	 * The `source` enum is deliberately narrow: stderr-pattern and
436	 * exit-code detection were retired after producing false-positive
437	 * pauses. Narrow-then-expand — new sources can be added once there is
438	 * real data calling for them.
439	 */
440	export interface SessionTerminationTag {
441	  /** Reason discriminator. */
442	  kind: 'rate-limit';
443	  /** Which provider signal fired the detection. */
444	  source: 'ndjson-result';
445	  /** Optional operator-facing explanation from the provider. */
446	  detail?: string;
447	}
448	
449	/**
450	 * Passive diagnostic record captured by the session provider when a
451	 * session terminates with `status: 'failed'` (non-zero exit, no
452	 * structured termination tag, no cancel override). Captured solely so
453	 * operators can audit the signal that did NOT satisfy the narrowed
454	 * rate-limit detector — the Animator's back-off machine never widens
455	 * its pause gate on this field, only displays/logs it.
456	 *
457	 * Carries the process exit code and, when available, the last 200
458	 * characters of the subprocess stderr stream. Not produced for
459	 * `'timeout'`, `'cancelled'`, `'rate-limited'` or `'completed'`
460	 * sessions.
461	 */
462	export interface TerminationDiagnostic {
463	  /** Process exit code that produced the `'failed'` status. */
464	  exitCode: number;
465	  /** Tail of the subprocess stderr (<= 200 chars) if any was captured. */
466	  stderrExcerpt?: string;
467	}
468	
469	export interface SessionProviderResult {
470	  /** Exit status. */
471	  status: 'completed' | 'failed' | 'timeout' | 'cancelled' | 'rate-limited';
472	  /**
473	   * Structured termination tag. Providers set this when the terminal
474	   * status was produced by a specific detected condition (today: rate
475	   * limiting). Absent when the terminal status has no extra structure.
476	   */
477	  terminationTag?: SessionTerminationTag;
478	  /** Numeric exit code from the process. */
479	  exitCode: number;
480	  /** Error message if failed. */
481	  error?: string;
482	  /** Provider's session id (e.g. for --resume). */
483	  providerSessionId?: string;
484	  /** Token usage, if the provider can report it. */
485	  tokenUsage?: TokenUsage;
486	  /** Cost in USD, if the provider can report it. */
487	  costUsd?: number;
488	  /**
489	   * The session's full transcript — array of NDJSON message objects.
490	   *
491	   * In attached mode, this is populated directly from the NDJSON stream.
492	   * In detached mode, the transcript is written to SQLite by the babysitter
493	   * and is available via `stacks.book('animator', 'transcripts').get(sessionId)`
494	   * rather than on this result object.
495	   */
496	  transcript?: TranscriptMessage[];
497	  /**
498	   * The final assistant text from the session.
499	   * Extracted from the last assistant message's text content blocks.
500	   * Undefined if the session produced no assistant output.
501	   */
502	  output?: string;
503	  /** Process signal name if the session was killed by signal (e.g. 'SIGTERM', 'SIGKILL'). */
504	  signal?: string;
505	}
506	
507	// ── Cross-process cancellation handle ───────────────────────────────
508	
509	/**
510	 * Provider-owned opaque handle for cross-process cancellation. The
511	 * discriminated union of one variant the codebase actually implements
512	 * end-to-end today: a local process group identified by its PGID. New
513	 * variants (container, remote) get added when the corresponding backend
514	 * lands — pre-typing fictive future shapes obscures which kinds the
515	 * runtime can actually interpret.
516	 *
517	 * The Animator does not interpret the handle — it passes it back to the
518	 * provider's `cancel()` method when cancellation is requested. The
519	 * `kind` discriminator lets the provider's `cancel()` switch on the
520	 * variant exhaustively (see `claude-code/src/index.ts`).
521	 */
522	export type LocalPgidHandle = { kind: 'local-pgid'; pgid: number };
523	
524	export type CancelHandle = LocalPgidHandle;
525	
526	// ── Stacks document type ─────────────────────────────────────────────
527	
528	/**
529	 * The session document stored in The Stacks' `sessions` book.
530	 * Includes all SessionResult fields plus the `id` required by BookEntry.
531	 */
532	export interface SessionDoc {
533	  id: string;
534	  /**
535	   * Session status. Initially written as `'running'` when the session is
536	   * launched, then updated to a terminal status (`'completed'`, `'failed'`,
537	   * `'timeout'`, or `'cancelled'`) after the provider exits or cancel is called.
538	   * The `'running'` state is transient.
539	   * `SessionResult.status` only includes terminal states.
540	   */
541	  status: 'pending' | 'running' | 'completed' | 'failed' | 'timeout' | 'cancelled' | 'rate-limited';
542	  startedAt: string;
543	  endedAt?: string;
544	  durationMs?: number;
545	  provider: string;
546	  exitCode?: number;
547	  error?: string;
548	  conversationId?: string;
549	  providerSessionId?: string;
550	  tokenUsage?: TokenUsage;
551	  costUsd?: number;
552	  metadata?: Record<string, unknown>;
553	  /** The final assistant text from the session. */
554	  output?: string;
555	  /**
556	   * Provider-owned opaque handle for cross-process cancellation.
557	   * Written by the Animator from the provider's processInfo at session
558	   * launch. See `CancelHandle` for the discriminated-union catalog of
559	   * shapes the runtime can interpret.
560	   */
561	  cancelHandle?: CancelHandle;
562	  /**
563	   * ISO timestamp of the last lifecycle signal from the session host.
564	   *
565	   * Updated on: pending pre-write, ready report (session-running),
566	   * heartbeat, terminal report (session-record). The guild writes its
567	   * own wall-clock time — never a host-supplied timestamp.
568	   *
569	   * The reconciler uses this to detect dead sessions:
570	   *   if (now - lastActivityAt - downtimeCredit > stalenessThreshold) → failed
571	   *
572	   * See: docs/architecture/detached-sessions.md § Reconciliation
573	   */
574	  lastActivityAt?: string;
575	  /**
576	   * Tool names this session is authorized to call over the Tool HTTP API.
577	   *
578	   * Source of truth for the daemon's Stacks-backed authorize callback.
579	   * Pre-written by `launchDetached()` (or animate()) before the babysitter
580	   * is spawned, then preserved through subsequent updates.
581	   */
582	  authorizedTools?: string[];
583	  /**
584	   * Structured termination tag, mirroring SessionResult.terminationTag.
585	   * Persisted on the SessionDoc so callers that read sessions out of The
586	   * Stacks (Spider's tryCollect, UI inspectors, the Laboratory CDC
587	   * stream) can tell why the session terminated without re-running the
588	   * provider's cascade. Set when the provider emits one via
589	   * `session-record`, preserved across status patches.
590	   */
591	  terminationTag?: SessionTerminationTag;
592	  /**
593	   * Passive diagnostic captured when the session ended with
594	   * `status: 'failed'` and no structured termination tag. Shape is
595	   * `{ exitCode, stderrExcerpt? }`. Informational only — the Animator's
596	   * back-off machine does not consume this field. See TerminationDiagnostic.
597	   */
598	  terminationDiagnostic?: TerminationDiagnostic;
599	  /** Index signature required by BookEntry. */
600	  [key: string]: unknown;
601	}
602	
603	/**
604	 * The transcript document stored in The Stacks' `transcripts` book.
605	 * One record per session — 1:1 relationship with SessionDoc.
606	 */
607	export interface TranscriptDoc {
608	  /** Same as the session id. */
609	  id: string;
610	  /** Full NDJSON transcript from the session. */
611	  messages: TranscriptMessage[];
612	  /** Index signature required by BookEntry. */
613	  [key: string]: unknown;
614	}
615	
616	/**
617	 * Operational state stored in the Animator's 'state' book.
618	 * Single well-known document with id 'guild-heartbeat'.
619	 */
620	export interface GuildStateDoc {
621	  id: string;
622	  /** ISO timestamp of the last guild self-heartbeat. */
623	  guildAliveAt: string;
624	  /** Index signature required by BookEntry. */
625	  [key: string]: unknown;
626	}
627	
628	// ── Animator status doc (rate-limit back-off) ────────────────────────
629	
630	/**
631	 * Reason for the Animator's current pause state. Extensible — today only
632	 * `'rate-limit'` is produced, but the field is open so future operator-
633	 * triggered pause can reuse the same doc shape without a schema bump.
634	 */
635	export type AnimatorPauseReason = 'rate-limit';
636	
637	/**
638	 * Operational state stored in the Animator's 'status' book.
639	 *
640	 * Single well-known document with id 'current'. Tracks whether the
641	 * anima provider is currently rate-limited, when the current pause
642	 * window ends, and the exponential back-off level that will be applied
643	 * to the next pause.
644	 *
645	 * Consumers compose against the canonical `isDispatchable(doc)` helper
646	 * exported from this package — TypeScript callers (Spider's crawl gate,
647	 * the `animator-paused` block-type, the in-process `animate()`
648	 * pre-check) import it directly; non-TS callers (the Oculus banner)
649	 * read the server-computed `dispatchable` boolean enriched onto the
650	 * `animator-status` tool / `/api/animator/status` route response. The
651	 * canonical predicate tolerates the "stale running" edge cases — a
652	 * daemon restart leaves the doc in its last persisted state; the first
653	 * successful dispatch naturally flips it back to `'running'` (D24).
654	 */
655	export interface AnimatorStatusDoc {
656	  id: string;
657	  /** Current pause state. `'running'` means sessions dispatch normally. */
658	  state: 'running' | 'paused';
659	  /** ISO timestamp when the current pause began. Absent when running. */
660	  pausedSince?: string;
661	  /**
662	   * ISO timestamp when the current pause window is due to clear.
663	   * Consumers should call `isDispatchable(doc)` rather than combining
664	   * this with `state` directly. Absent when running.
665	   */
666	  pausedUntil?: string;
667	  /** Reason for the current pause. Absent when running. */
668	  pauseReason?: AnimatorPauseReason;
669	  /**
670	   * Current back-off level. Starts at 0 for the first pause; only
671	   * increments when a rate-limit hit arrives *after* a resume attempt
672	   * has dispatched. Reset to 0 on any non-rate-limit terminal. (D7, D8)
673	   */
674	  backoffLevel: number;
675	  /** ISO timestamp of the most recent rate-limit hit, across all pauses. */
676	  backoffLastHitAt?: string;
677	  /** Session id that produced the current (or most recent) pause. */
678	  lastTriggeringSession?: string;
679	  /** Index signature required by BookEntry. */
680	  [key: string]: unknown;
681	}
682	
683	// ── Animator config ──────────────────────────────────────────────────
684	
685	/**
686	 * Rate-limit back-off configuration — lives under
687	 * `guild.json["animator"]["rateLimit"]["backoff"]`.
688	 *
689	 * Validated fail-loud at animator startup (D10 — patron override of the
690	 * silent-default convention applied *only* to this block). The block is
691	 * optional; missing values fall back to the defaults documented below.
692	 */
693	export interface AnimatorRateLimitBackoffConfig {
694	  /**
695	   * Initial pause duration in milliseconds (back-off level 0). Must be a
696	   * positive integer. Default: 15 minutes (900_000).
697	   */
698	  initialMs?: number;
699	  /**
700	   * Upper bound for the pause window after exponential back-off. Must be
701	   * a positive integer greater than or equal to `initialMs`. Default:
702	   * 1 hour (3_600_000).
703	   */
704	  maxMs?: number;
705	  /**
706	   * Multiplier applied per successive failed resume attempt. Must be a
707	   * finite number greater than 1. Default: 2.
708	   */
709	  factor?: number;
710	}
711	
712	/**
713	 * Umbrella for all rate-limit-related Animator configuration. Today the
714	 * only nested block is `backoff`; the wrapper is in place so future
715	 * rate-limit tuning (detection thresholds, per-source overrides, etc.)
716	 * can land without flattening the top-level namespace.
717	 */
718	export interface AnimatorRateLimitConfig {
719	  /** Rate-limit back-off tuning. See AnimatorRateLimitBackoffConfig. */
720	  backoff?: AnimatorRateLimitBackoffConfig;
721	}
722	
723	/** Plugin configuration stored at guild.json["animator"]. */
724	export interface AnimatorConfig {
725	  /**
726	   * Plugin id of the apparatus that implements AnimatorSessionProvider.
727	   * The Animator looks this up via guild().apparatus() at animate-time.
728	   * Defaults to 'claude-code' if not specified.
729	   */
730	  sessionProvider?: string;
731	  /**
732	   * Rate-limit configuration umbrella. Optional; each nested field falls
733	   * back to its documented default when absent. Malformed values throw
734	   * at startup.
735	   */
736	  rateLimit?: AnimatorRateLimitConfig;
737	}
738	
739	// Augment GuildConfig so `guild().guildConfig().animator` is typed without
740	// requiring a manual type parameter at the call site.
741	declare module '@shardworks/nexus-core' {
742	  interface GuildConfig {
743	    animator?: AnimatorConfig;
744	  }
745	}

content.content:
1	/**
2	 * Reckoner public types.
3	 *
4	 * The Reckoner is the petitioner-scheduler apparatus. It owns the
5	 * kit-static petitioner registry, exposes the canonical
6	 * `petition()` / `withdraw()` helpers (Workflow 2 in the contract
7	 * document), and surfaces inspection helpers for downstream
8	 * consumers (the periodic tick handler, the vision-keeper kit, the
9	 * patron-bridge apparatus).
10	 *
11	 * This file is the single source of public-symbol truth — every
12	 * type a downstream import touches lives here, including
13	 * `ReckonerApi`. The factory in `./reckoner.ts` builds a value
14	 * matching `ReckonerApi`; see that module for how `provides` is
15	 * assembled.
16	 *
17	 * See: docs/architecture/petitioner-registration.md  (the load-
18	 * bearing contract document) and
19	 * docs/architecture/apparatus/reckoner.md (the apparatus shape).
20	 */
21	
22	import type { WritDoc } from '@shardworks/clerk-apparatus';
23	
24	// ── Priority ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
25	
26	/**
27	 * The five-dimensional priority shape declared on every petition.
28	 *
29	 * Each dimension answers a different question; the Reckoner
30	 * combines them at consideration time. See §3 of the contract
31	 * document for the semantics of every value.
32	 */
33	export interface Priority {
34	  /** Relationship to the product vision (vision-blocker, vision-violator, vision-advancer, vision-neutral). */
35	  visionRelation:
36	    | 'vision-blocker'
37	    | 'vision-violator'
38	    | 'vision-advancer'
39	    | 'vision-neutral';
40	  /** Magnitude axis (critical, serious, moderate, minor). */
41	  severity: 'critical' | 'serious' | 'moderate' | 'minor';
42	  /** What fraction of the system is affected (whole-product, major-area, minor-area). */
43	  scope: 'whole-product' | 'major-area' | 'minor-area';
44	  /** Two genuinely-independent time-pressure axes. */
45	  time: {
46	    /** Drift sentinel / accumulating-debt flag. */
47	    decay: boolean;
48	    /** Hard deadline if any (ISO date string), else `null`. */
49	    deadline: string | null;
50	  };
51	  /** Multi-valued classification tag set. NOT a priority axis — describes what kind of work this is. */
52	  domain: Array<
53	    | 'security'
54	    | 'compliance'
55	    | 'cost'
56	    | 'feature'
57	    | 'quality'
58	    | 'infrastructure'
59	    | 'documentation'
60	    | 'research'
61	    | 'ergonomics'
62	  >;
63	}
64	
65	/** Allowed values for `Priority.visionRelation`. */
66	export const VISION_RELATION_VALUES = [
67	  'vision-blocker',
68	  'vision-violator',
69	  'vision-advancer',
70	  'vision-neutral',
71	] as const;
72	
73	/** Allowed values for `Priority.severity`. */
74	export const SEVERITY_VALUES = [
75	  'critical',
76	  'serious',
77	  'moderate',
78	  'minor',
79	] as const;
80	
81	/** Allowed values for `Priority.scope`. */
82	export const SCOPE_VALUES = [
83	  'whole-product',
84	  'major-area',
85	  'minor-area',
86	] as const;
87	
88	/** Allowed values for `Priority.domain[]`. */
89	export const DOMAIN_VALUES = [
90	  'security',
91	  'compliance',
92	  'cost',
93	  'feature',
94	  'quality',
95	  'infrastructure',
96	  'documentation',
97	  'research',
98	  'ergonomics',
99	] as const;
100	
101	// ── Complexity ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
102	
103	/**
104	 * Petitioner-side coarse cost estimate. See §4 of the contract
105	 * document for the calibration ranges. Refined by the Astrolabe at
106	 * plan time; this exists for early-stage trade-offs only.
107	 */
108	export type ComplexityTier =
109	  | 'mechanical'
110	  | 'bounded'
111	  | 'exploratory'
112	  | 'open-ended';
113	
114	// ── Reckoner ext shape ────────────────────────────────────────────────
115	
116	/**
117	 * Shape of `writ.ext['reckoner']` — the contract slot a petitioner
118	 * stamps onto a writ to opt it into Reckoner consideration.
119	 *
120	 * The Reckoner sweeps the writs book on every periodic tick and
121	 * treats every writ carrying this slot in `new` phase as a held
122	 * petition. Petitioners can stamp it directly via `clerk.post()` +
123	 * `clerk.setWritExt()` (Workflow 1) or use the `petition()` helper
124	 * (Workflow 2) for default-fill and validation; both paths produce
125	 * the same on-disk shape.
126	 */
127	export interface ReckonerExt {
128	  /** Identifies the petitioner. Must be `{pluginId}.{kebab-suffix}`. */
129	  source: string;
130	  /** Fully-defaulted multi-dimensional priority. */
131	  priority: Priority;
132	  /** Optional petitioner-side coarse cost estimate. */
133	  complexity?: ComplexityTier;
134	  /** Opaque petitioner-defined data; the Reckoner stores but does not introspect. */
135	  payload?: unknown;
136	  /** Additive non-priority metadata (multi-instance discrimination, observability hints). */
137	  labels?: Record<string, string>;
138	}
139	
140	// ── Petition request ──────────────────────────────────────────────────
141	
142	/**
143	 * Ext-only argument shape — the `writ.ext['reckoner']` fields a
144	 * petitioner supplies, decoupled from the writ-shape fields.
145	 *
146	 * Used as the second argument to the stamp-only form of
147	 * `ReckonerApi.petition(writId, extRequest)` (the draft-then-publish
148	 * idiom: post a writ in its initial phase without `ext.reckoner`,
149	 * wire dependencies, then stamp `ext.reckoner` to publish). The
150	 * create+stamp form of `petition()` accepts a `PetitionRequest`
151	 * which extends this shape with the writ-shape fields.
152	 *
153	 * The helper takes a partial priority — every omitted dimension is
154	 * filled with the contract default at the helper boundary (D15) so
155	 * petitioners with one strongly-felt dimension do not need to
156	 * supply the entire shape.
157	 */
158	export interface PetitionExtRequest {
159	  /** Petitioner source id. Required. Matched against the registry. */
160	  source: string;
161	  /** Partial priority — omitted dimensions fall back to defaults at the helper boundary. */
162	  priority?: Partial<Priority>;
163	  /** Optional coarse complexity estimate. */
164	  complexity?: ComplexityTier;
165	  /** Opaque petitioner-defined data. */
166	  payload?: unknown;
167	  /** Additive metadata labels. */
168	  labels?: Record<string, string>;
169	}
170	
171	/**
172	 * Argument shape for the create+stamp form of `ReckonerApi.petition()`.
173	 *
174	 * Extends `PetitionExtRequest` with the writ-shape fields that pass
175	 * straight through to `clerk.post()`. The optional `type` mirrors
176	 * `PostCommissionRequest.type?` exactly — when omitted, the guild's
177	 * default writ type is used (D21).
178	 */
179	export interface PetitionRequest extends PetitionExtRequest {
180	  // ── writ fields (passed through to clerk.post) ───────────────────
181	  /** Writ type. Defaults to the guild's configured default writ type. */
182	  type?: string;
183	  /** Short human-readable title describing the petition. */
184	  title: string;
185	  /** Detail text. */
186	  body: string;
187	  /** Optional target codex name. */
188	  codex?: string;
189	  /** Create this writ as a child of the specified parent writ. */
190	  parentId?: string;
191	}
192	
193	// ── Petitioner descriptor ─────────────────────────────────────────────
194	
195	/**
196	 * Kit-contributed petitioner descriptor.
197	 *
198	 * Petitioners declare themselves via a `petitioners` kit
199	 * contribution — an array of these objects under that key on a kit
200	 * (or apparatus supportKit). The Reckoner consumes the array at
201	 * boot, validates each entry's source-id grammar, and seals the
202	 * registry at `phase:started`.
203	 *
204	 * The contract floor is intentionally minimal (D19, patron
205	 * override): only `source` and `description`. Enrichment fields
206	 * (contributing plugin id, timestamps, etc.) wait for a named
207	 * consumer.
208	 */
209	export interface PetitionerDescriptor {
210	  /** Fully-qualified source id of the form `{pluginId}.{kebab-suffix}`. */
211	  source: string;
212	  /** Human-readable description of what this petitioner emits. */
213	  description: string;
214	}
215	
216	// ── Reckonings record ────────────────────────────────────────────────
217	
218	/**
219	 * Outcome enum for a Reckonings record. The tick handler emits
220	 * `'accepted'` (after a successful `new → active` transition),
221	 * `'declined'` (after a `new → cancelled` transition driven by the
222	 * source-unregistered, source-banned, or scheduler-decline path),
223	 * and `'deferred'` (when the active scheduler returns a `defer`
224	 * decision; the writ stays in `new`). The fourth value, `'no-op'`,
225	 * is reserved for future commissions.
226	 *
227	 * See: docs/architecture/reckonings-book.md §"Outcome enum".
228	 */
229	export type ReckoningOutcome = 'accepted' | 'deferred' | 'declined' | 'no-op';
230	
231	/**
232	 * Decline-reason enum for a Reckonings record with `outcome: 'declined'`.
233	 * The v0 handler emits only `'source_unregistered'`; the wider set is
234	 * declared here so consumer types can pattern-match without drifting
235	 * when later commissions add reason paths.
236	 *
237	 * See: docs/architecture/reckonings-book.md §"Decline reasons".
238	 */
239	export type ReckoningDeclineReason =
240	  | 'malformed'
241	  | 'duplicate'
242	  | 'policy_violation'
243	  | 'source_banned'
244	  | 'source_unregistered'
245	  | 'other';
246	
247	/**
248	 * Defer-reason enum for a Reckonings record with `outcome: 'deferred'`.
249	 *
250	 * The `dependency_pending` and `dependency_failed` reasons are emitted
251	 * by the dependency-aware consideration gate (D1–D13 of the
252	 * dependency-aware-consideration commission): a held petition with one
253	 * or more outbound `depends-on` links whose targets are not all
254	 * `cleared` (terminal + success-or-cancelled attrs) is left in `new`
255	 * phase and a deferred Reckonings row is written. `dependency_failed`
256	 * carries failed-precedence — when at least one dependency target
257	 * resolves to a `failed` classification, the gate emits
258	 * `dependency_failed` regardless of how many other deps are still
259	 * gating. The scheduler-emitted defer path now writes deferred rows
260	 * with `'other'` as the reason (the decision's lineage string lands
261	 * in `deferNote`). The remaining values (`priority`, `queue_depth`,
262	 * `time_hold`, `patron_policy`) remain reserved for future
263	 * commissions.
264	 *
265	 * See: docs/architecture/reckonings-book.md §"Defer reasons" and
266	 * docs/architecture/apparatus/reckoner.md §"Dependency-aware defer".
267	 */
268	export type ReckoningDeferReason =
269	  | 'priority'
270	  | 'queue_depth'
271	  | 'time_hold'
272	  | 'patron_policy'
273	  | 'dependency_pending'
274	  | 'dependency_failed'
275	  | 'other';
276	
277	/**
278	 * Vision-relation projection on a Reckonings record. Mirrors the
279	 * `Priority.visionRelation` enum exactly — projected at the top level
280	 * of the record so the per-vision-relation timeline index can name it
281	 * directly.
282	 */
283	export type ReckoningVisionRelation = Priority['visionRelation'];
284	
285	/**
286	 * Severity projection on a Reckonings record. Mirrors the
287	 * `Priority.severity` enum exactly — projected at the top level of
288	 * the record so the per-severity timeline index can name it directly.
289	 */
290	export type ReckoningSeverity = Priority['severity'];
291	
292	/**
293	 * One row in the Reckonings book — the Reckoner's evaluation journal.
294	 *
295	 * Every meaningful consideration produces one record. A record
296	 * with `outcome: 'accepted'` corresponds to a `new → active` phase
297	 * transition; `'declined'` to a `new → cancelled` transition; the
298	 * other two outcomes are reserved for future commissions.
299	 *
300	 * The flat optional layout (every reason field at the top level)
301	 * intentionally trades type-purity for index-friendliness — the
302	 * architecture doc notes the iff-outcome invariant is writer-enforced
303	 * by the Reckoner and consumer types decode against a discriminated
304	 * union. See `docs/architecture/reckonings-book.md` §"Record body".
305	 */
306	export interface ReckoningDoc {
307	  /** Index signature required to satisfy the Stacks `BookEntry` constraint. */
308	  [key: string]: unknown;
309	  /** Unique id (`rk-<base36_ts>-<hex>`). Sortable by creation time. */
310	  id: string;
311	  /** The Clerk writ this record is about (the held petition). */
312	  writId: string;
313	  /**
314	   * Forward-compatible extension to the contract shape: the
315	   * triggering writ's `updatedAt` value, captured at consideration
316	   * time. Used for the `(writId, writUpdatedAt)` dedupe identity
317	   * (D6/D23). Not declared in `reckonings-book.md`'s illustrative
318	   * schema; the doc's "every meaningful field named and filterable"
319	   * ethos justifies storing it as a top-level field rather than
320	   * burying it under a context blob.
321	   */
322	  writUpdatedAt: string;
323	  /** Lean projection: `ext.reckoner.source`. */
324	  source: string;
325	  /** Lean projection: `ext.reckoner.priority.visionRelation`. */
326	  visionRelation: ReckoningVisionRelation;
327	  /** Lean projection: `ext.reckoner.priority.severity`. */
328	  severity: ReckoningSeverity;
329	  /** Outcome enum — drives the discriminated-union reason fields. */
330	  outcome: ReckoningOutcome;
331	  /**
332	   * Triggering Clockworks event id, stamped from the
333	   * `clockworks.timer` event the standing-order dispatch handed to
334	   * the tick relay. Populated whenever the relay handler receives a
335	   * non-null event with an id; omitted on test paths driving the
336	   * pure tick helper directly with `null`. Synthesizing a fallback
337	   * id would pollute rows with non-joinable values; absence is
338	   * meaningful and is what test rows look like.
339	   */
340	  tickEventId?: string;
341	  /** ISO timestamp when the Reckoner completed this consideration. */
342	  consideredAt: string;
343	  // ── Outcome-keyed reason metadata (flat optionals, writer-enforced) ──
344	  /** Populated iff `outcome === 'declined'`. */
345	  declineReason?: ReckoningDeclineReason;
346	  /** Optional remediation hint accompanying a decline. */
347	  remediationHint?: string;
348	  /** Populated iff `outcome === 'deferred'`. */
349	  deferReason?: ReckoningDeferReason;
350	  /** Optional defer-until ISO timestamp. */
351	  deferUntil?: string;
352	  /** Optional defer wake-up event pattern. */
353	  deferSignal?: string;
354	  /** Running deferral counter for this writ. */
355	  deferCount?: number;
356	  /** First-seen-as-deferred ISO timestamp. */
357	  firstDeferredAt?: string;
358	  /** Most-recent deferral ISO timestamp. */
359	  lastDeferredAt?: string;
360	  /** Optional freeform short note on a deferral. */
361	  deferNote?: string;
362	  /**
363	   * Optional scheduler-emitted weight projected onto the row when a
364	   * `SchedulerDecision` carried one. Forward-compatible with
365	   * future weighted-priority schedulers; absent for the v0
366	   * always-approve scheduler. The Reckoner's row writer threads the
367	   * value through verbatim — no normalization, no defaulting.
368	   */
369	  weight?: number;
370	}
371	
372	// ── Scheduler ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
373	
374	/**
375	 * A held writ — a writ in `new` phase carrying `ext.reckoner`.
376	 *
377	 * Vocabulary alias for `WritDoc` used at scheduler-input read sites
378	 * to make intent explicit. There is no runtime invariant beyond what
379	 * the apparatus already guarantees at the call site (phase + ext
380	 * gates run before the scheduler is invoked); the alias is purely
381	 * documentary so a reader of `SchedulerInput` knows the candidate
382	 * shape without chasing back through the rule sequence.
383	 */
384	export type HeldWrit = WritDoc;
385	
386	/**
387	 * Forward-compatible capacity slot threaded into `SchedulerInput`.
388	 *
389	 * Empty in v0 — the slot exists so the scheduler interface does not
390	 * have to grow a new positional argument when a future commission
391	 * adds capacity tracking (concurrent-active counts, per-source
392	 * quotas, queue-depth observations). Schedulers that do not consume
393	 * capacity simply ignore the field.
394	 */
395	export interface CapacitySnapshot {
396	  /** Reserved for future capacity-tracking commissions. v0 ships no fields. */
397	  [key: string]: unknown;
398	}
399	
400	/**
401	 * The outcome a scheduler emits for a held writ. Mirrors the three
402	 * substantive Reckonings outcomes:
403	 *
404	 * - `'approve'` — drive the writ out of `new` to its type's active
405	 *   target and append an `accepted` Reckonings row.
406	 * - `'defer'`   — leave the writ in `new`. No transition, no row
407	 *   in v0 (deferred rows require richer reason metadata than the
408	 *   `SchedulerDecision` shape declares).
409	 * - `'decline'` — drive the writ to `cancelled` with the decision's
410	 *   `reason` recorded as the resolution string and append a
411	 *   `declined` Reckonings row carrying `declineReason: 'other'` plus
412	 *   the reason in `remediationHint`.
413	 */
414	export type SchedulerOutcome = 'approve' | 'defer' | 'decline';
415	
416	/**
417	 * One scheduler decision targeting one held writ.
418	 *
419	 * `writId` identifies the candidate; `outcome` selects the
420	 * disposition; `reason` is a human-readable lineage string the
421	 * apparatus persists alongside the decision (resolution string for
422	 * declines, `remediationHint` for declined Reckonings rows, or a
423	 * grep-able marker on accepted rows). `weight` is an optional
424	 * scheduler-emitted score the apparatus threads onto the resulting
425	 * Reckonings row when present.
426	 */
427	export interface SchedulerDecision {
428	  /** The held writ this decision applies to. Must match a candidate from the input. */
429	  writId: string;
430	  /** The scheduler's selected outcome. */
431	  outcome: SchedulerOutcome;
432	  /** Human-readable lineage. Persisted on Reckonings rows where applicable. */
433	  reason: string;
434	  /** Optional scheduler-emitted weight. Threaded through to the Reckonings row when present. */
435	  weight?: number;
436	}
437	
438	/**
439	 * Argument shape for `Scheduler.evaluate()`.
440	 *
441	 * The Reckoner samples `now` once at the call boundary so the row id
442	 * and `consideredAt` stay consistent within a single consideration
443	 * (D33). `config` is the validated, scheduler-narrowed view of the
444	 * `reckoner.schedulerConfig` block — the apparatus runs
445	 * `validateConfig` immediately before each `evaluate` call (D17) so
446	 * each invocation sees the freshest config.
447	 */
448	export interface SchedulerInput<TConfig = unknown> {
449	  /** The held writs the scheduler is being asked to consider. */
450	  candidates: readonly HeldWrit[];
451	  /** Forward-compatible capacity slot. Empty in v0. */
452	  capacity: CapacitySnapshot;
453	  /** Sampling timestamp from the apparatus call boundary. */
454	  now: Date;
455	  /** Validated config slice — narrowed by `Scheduler.validateConfig` when present. */
456	  config: TConfig;
457	}
458	
459	/**
460	 * A scheduler — pluggable selection policy contributed via the
461	 * `schedulers` kit-contribution type.
462	 *
463	 * Each registered scheduler declares an `id` of the form
464	 * `{contributingPluginId}.{kebab-suffix}`, a human-readable
465	 * `description`, an `evaluate` function that takes a
466	 * `SchedulerInput` and returns one or more `SchedulerDecision`s, and
467	 * an optional `validateConfig` narrower the apparatus calls per
468	 * evaluation when the operator has supplied a `reckoner.schedulerConfig`
469	 * block. The Reckoner resolves a single active scheduler at startup
470	 * from `guild.json reckoner.scheduler` (defaults to
471	 * `reckoner.always-approve` when unset).
472	 *
473	 * Schedulers reach for shared guild state (Stacks book handles, Clerk
474	 * helpers) via `guild()` rather than constructor injection — the
475	 * direct-instance shape mirrors Fabricator's `EngineDesign` registry
476	 * precedent.
477	 */
478	export interface Scheduler<TConfig = unknown> {
479	  /** Fully-qualified id of the form `{pluginId}.{kebab-suffix}`. */
480	  id: string;
481	  /** Human-readable description of the scheduling policy. */
482	  description: string;
483	  /** Run the policy against the candidate set and emit decisions. */
484	  evaluate(input: SchedulerInput<TConfig>): Promise<readonly SchedulerDecision[]>;
485	  /**
486	   * Optional config narrower. Called per evaluation immediately
487	   * before `evaluate`; throws are caught by the apparatus, logged
488	   * fail-loud, and skip the call without writing a row or
489	   * transitioning the writ.
490	   */
491	  validateConfig?(raw: unknown): TConfig;
492	}
493	
494	// ── Reckoner config ───────────────────────────────────────────────────
495	
496	/**
497	 * Reckoner apparatus configuration — lives under the `reckoner`
498	 * key in `guild.json`.
499	 *
500	 * Both fields are optional. Missing config is treated as defaults
501	 * (`enforceRegistration: false`, `disabledSources: []`). When the
502	 * block is present, type mismatches throw fail-loud at the read
503	 * site (D12).
504	 */
505	export interface ReckonerConfig {
506	  /**
507	   * Controls how the Reckoner handles petitions whose source is
508	   * not in the registry. `false` (default) — log a warning and
509	   * proceed. `true` — decline the petition fail-loud at the
510	   * helper boundary.
511	   */
512	  enforceRegistration?: boolean;
513	  /**
514	   * Per-source disable list. Petitions from any source in this
515	   * array are skipped (left in `new` phase). The list is re-read
516	   * on each call so operators can hot-edit `guild.json` without a
517	   * restart (D20).
518	   */
519	  disabledSources?: string[];
520	  /**
521	   * Selector for the active scheduler. Must match a registered
522	   * `Scheduler.id`; resolved once at `phase:started` and cached for
523	   * the seal's life. When unset, defaults to the built-in
524	   * `reckoner.always-approve` scheduler. When set to an unknown id,
525	   * the Reckoner fail-loud throws at startup with a diagnostic
526	   * listing every registered id.
527	   */
528	  scheduler?: string;
529	  /**
530	   * Opaque per-evaluation config slice passed into the active
531	   * scheduler. Re-read from `guild.json` on every consideration so
532	   * operators can hot-edit (D17) without a restart. The Reckoner
533	   * does not narrow this value — each scheduler's `validateConfig`
534	   * is the boundary.
535	   */
536	  schedulerConfig?: unknown;
537	}
538	
539	// Augment GuildConfig so `guild().guildConfig().reckoner` is typed
540	// without requiring a manual type parameter at the call site.
541	declare module '@shardworks/nexus-core' {
542	  interface GuildConfig {
543	    reckoner?: ReckonerConfig;
544	  }
545	}
546	
547	// ── API surface ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────
548	
549	/**
550	 * The Reckoner's runtime API — retrieved via
551	 * `guild().apparatus<ReckonerApi>('reckoner')`.
552	 *
553	 * The surface is a coherent set of helpers for petitioner authoring
554	 * (`petition`, `withdraw`) and registry inspection
555	 * (`isSourceRegistered`, `isSourceDisabled`, `listPetitioners`).
556	 * The periodic tick handler consumes the inspection helpers
557	 * per-tick; the petitioner authoring helpers are the canonical
558	 * Workflow-2 path for petitioners.
559	 */
560	export interface ReckonerApi {
561	  /**
562	   * Create + stamp form. Post a writ in its registered initial
563	   * phase with `writ.ext['reckoner']` set correctly.
564	   *
565	   * Resolves the source against the registry. When the source is
566	   * not registered:
567	   *   - `enforceRegistration: true` — throws fail-loud, no writ
568	   *     is created.
569	   *   - `enforceRegistration: false` (default) — logs a warning
570	   *     and proceeds.
571	   *
572	   * Validates every priority dimension against its enum. Applies
573	   * defaults to omitted priority dimensions (field-by-field
574	   * merge). Delegates to the stamp-only form below for the actual
575	   * ext write — source/priority validation runs once via the
576	   * shared internal helper consumed by both forms, so a malformed
577	   * input never produces an orphan writ.
578	   *
579	   * Returns the resulting writ document (post-`setWritExt`, so
580	   * `writ.ext.reckoner` is populated on the returned shape).
581	   */
582	  petition(request: PetitionRequest): Promise<WritDoc>;
583	
584	  /**
585	   * Stamp-only form — the draft-then-publish idiom. Stamps
586	   * `writ.ext['reckoner']` onto an already-posted writ that is
587	   * still in its writ-type's initial phase, making the writ
588	   * Reckoner-visible. Mirrors the create+stamp form's source-
589	   * registry, priority-default-fill, and dimension-validation
590	   * semantics; differs only in that the caller has already
591	   * posted the writ (typically so dependencies via `clerk.link()`
592	   * could be wired before consideration).
593	   *
594	   * Guards (in order; each fails loud with a `[reckoner] petition:`
595	   * prefix and produces no writ mutation):
596	   *   1. source registry + `enforceRegistration` (same as create+stamp).
597	   *   2. priority dimension validation (same as create+stamp).
598	   *   3. writ exists (`clerk.show(writId)` throws otherwise).
599	   *   4. writ is in its type's initial phase (`clerk.isInitial(writ)`).
600	   *   5. `writ.ext.reckoner` is absent — petitioning is a one-time
601	   *      act; an already-petitioned writ never silently re-stamps.
602	   *
603	   * Composes naturally with `stacks.transaction(...)`: when the
604	   * petitioner wraps `clerk.post() + clerk.link() + reckoner.petition(
605	   * writId, ext)` in a single transaction, the writ becomes
606	   * Reckoner-visible only after the outer transaction commits.
607	   *
608	   * Returns the patched writ (post-`setWritExt`).
609	   */
610	  petition(writId: string, extRequest: PetitionExtRequest): Promise<WritDoc>;
611	
612	  /**
613	   * Withdraw a held writ by transitioning it to `cancelled`.
614	   *
615	   * Thin wrapper around `clerk.transition(writId, 'cancelled',
616	   * { resolution: reason })`. No source check, no owner check,
617	   * no ext check. Reason is passed through verbatim — undefined
618	   * stays undefined (no fabricated default).
619	   */
620	  withdraw(writId: string, reason?: string): Promise<WritDoc>;
621	
622	  /**
623	   * Return `true` when `source` is in the kit-static petitioner
624	   * registry. Reads the sealed in-memory registry; cheap to call.
625	   */
626	  isSourceRegistered(source: string): boolean;
627	
628	  /**
629	   * Return `true` when `source` is currently in the live
630	   * `disabledSources` config list. Re-reads `guild.json` on
631	   * every call so operators can hot-edit (D20).
632	   */
633	  isSourceDisabled(source: string): boolean;
634	
635	  /**
636	   * Return projections of every registered petitioner descriptor.
637	   *
638	   * Surfaces the contract floor only — `source` and `description`
639	   * — per D19. Returns an empty array when no petitioners are
640	   * registered.
641	   */
642	  listPetitioners(): PetitionerDescriptor[];
643	}
644

content.content:
1	/**
2	 * Lattice public types.
3	 *
4	 * All types exported from @shardworks/lattice-apparatus.
5	 *
6	 * The Lattice is the guild's notification substrate: a `lattice/pulses` book
7	 * that persists pulse records, a channel-factory contribution vocabulary
8	 * (`latticeChannels`) that lets external kits contribute push surfaces, and a
9	 * Phase 2 CDC dispatcher that fans newly-emitted pulses out to every
10	 * configured channel.
11	 *
12	 * Pulses are immutable event records — the only mutable field is
13	 * `deliveryState`, which the Lattice itself rewrites as dispatches progress.
14	 * There is no update or delete on the public API surface.
15	 */
16	
17	import type { BookEntry } from '@shardworks/stacks-apparatus';
18	
19	// ── Pulse document ───────────────────────────────────────────────────
20	
21	/**
22	 * Delivery state for a pulse.
23	 *
24	 *   - `pending`   — written but not yet dispatched to any channel.
25	 *   - `delivered` — every configured channel returned ok=true.
26	 *   - `failed`    — at least one configured channel returned ok=false or
27	 *                   threw; the Lattice records the failure and does not
28	 *                   retry (best-effort one-shot per D8).
29	 */
30	export type PulseDeliveryState = 'pending' | 'delivered' | 'failed';
31	
32	/**
33	 * A pulse document as stored in the `lattice/pulses` book.
34	 *
35	 * Pulses are emitted by observers (the Reckoner, future emitters) and
36	 * dispatched by the Lattice to every channel materialized from guild config.
37	 */
38	export interface PulseDoc extends BookEntry {
39	  /** Unique pulse id (`p-{base36_timestamp}-{hex_random}`). Sortable by creation time. */
40	  id: string;
41	  /**
42	   * Plugin id of the emitter that produced this pulse.
43	   *
44	   * The Lattice trusts the emitter to stamp its own source id — ownership
45	   * is convention-only (the Lattice does not forge or validate), mirroring
46	   * the plugin-keyed sub-slot convention used by `ClerkApi.setWritStatus`.
47	   */
48	  source: string;
49	  /**
50	   * Trigger type — the "what" axis of a pulse, independent of the source.
51	   *
52	   * Format: `{pluginId}.{kebab-suffix}` (e.g. `reckoner.writ-stuck`). Same
53	   * grammar Clerk uses for link kinds.
54	   */
55	  triggerType: string;
56	  /** Optional writ this pulse is about. Null for pulses that are not writ-scoped (e.g. queue-drained). */
57	  writId: string | null;
58	  /** Short human-readable title suitable for a notification headline. */
59	  title: string;
60	  /** Plain-text summary (one or two short lines). Degrades gracefully across channel backends. */
61	  summary: string;
62	  /**
63	   * Deep link into a surface that lets the patron act on the pulse.
64	   *
65	   * Always `null` in the MVP — no canonical public URL base exists yet.
66	   * Future commissions may populate this for pulses that have a sensible
67	   * remote-viewable URL.
68	   */
69	  linkUrl: string | null;
70	  /**
71	   * Structured payload keyed by triggerType. See individual emitters for the
72	   * concrete shapes (e.g. the Reckoner's writ-stuck context).
73	   */
74	  context: Record<string, unknown>;
75	  /** Current delivery state. Rewritten by the dispatcher. */
76	  deliveryState: PulseDeliveryState;
77	  /** Reason when `deliveryState` is `failed`. Absent on pending/delivered. */
78	  deliveryError?: string;
79	  /** ISO timestamp when the pulse was emitted. */
80	  createdAt: string;
81	  /** ISO timestamp of the last mutation. */
82	  updatedAt: string;
83	}
84	
85	// ── Channel abstraction ──────────────────────────────────────────────
86	
87	/**
88	 * Outcome returned by a channel's `send()`.
89	 *
90	 * Channels must never throw across the boundary — any error is reported as
91	 * `{ ok: false, error }` instead. This keeps the dispatcher simple and makes
92	 * the per-channel failure policy explicit.
93	 */
94	export type DeliveryOutcome =
95	  | { ok: true }
96	  | { ok: false; error: string };
97	
98	/**
99	 * A materialized channel instance.
100	 *
101	 * Channels are created once at startup from per-channel instance config and
102	 * re-used across dispatches. `type` is the factory id (e.g. `discord-webhook`).
103	 */
104	export interface LatticeChannel {
105	  /** Factory type id — matches `LatticeChannelFactory.type`. */
106	  readonly type: string;
107	  /**
108	   * Deliver a pulse. Must never throw across the boundary; any error is
109	   * reported as `{ ok: false, error }` instead.
110	   */
111	  send(pulse: PulseDoc): Promise<DeliveryOutcome>;
112	}
113	
114	/**
115	 * A channel factory contributed by a kit via `latticeChannels`.
116	 *
117	 * The factory builds a `LatticeChannel` from per-instance config drawn from
118	 * `guild.json`'s `lattice.channels` array. Instance config carries the
119	 * caller-provided fields (e.g. a `webhookUrlEnvVar` naming the env var
120	 * holding the Discord webhook URL) — secrets are resolved at send time,
121	 * never persisted.
122	 */
123	export interface LatticeChannelFactory {
124	  /** Factory type id. Must be unique across contributed factories. */
125	  readonly type: string;
126	  /**
127	   * Build a channel instance from the per-instance config drawn from
128	   * `lattice.channels[i]` (with the `type` field included).
129	   */
130	  create(instanceConfig: LatticeChannelInstanceConfig): LatticeChannel;
131	}
132	
133	/**
134	 * Per-channel instance config as it appears in `guild.json`'s
135	 * `lattice.channels` array. The `type` field selects the factory; all other
136	 * fields are factory-specific.
137	 */
138	export interface LatticeChannelInstanceConfig {
139	  /** Factory type id — selects the channel factory. */
140	  type: string;
141	  /** Caller-supplied instance fields (webhook env var, discord channel id, etc.). */
142	  [field: string]: unknown;
143	}
144	
145	// ── Kit contribution interface ───────────────────────────────────────
146	
147	/**
148	 * Kit contribution interface for plugins that extend the Lattice with
149	 * additional channel factories. Contribute an array of factories under
150	 * `latticeChannels`; each factory becomes selectable via its `type` field in
151	 * `lattice.channels`.
152	 */
153	export interface LatticeKit {
154	  /** Channel factories contributed by this kit. */
155	  latticeChannels?: LatticeChannelFactory[];
156	}
157	
158	// ── Config ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
159	
160	/**
161	 * Lattice apparatus configuration — lives under the `lattice` key in
162	 * `guild.json`.
163	 */
164	export interface LatticeConfig {
165	  /**
166	   * Configured channel instances. Each entry is passed to the factory whose
167	   * `type` field matches `entry.type`. Multiple entries may share a type —
168	   * e.g. two Discord webhook channels pointing at different endpoints.
169	   *
170	   * Secrets (webhook URLs, tokens, …) are never placed in this array
171	   * directly; instead entries name an env var (convention per-factory) that
172	   * is read at send time.
173	   */
174	  channels?: LatticeChannelInstanceConfig[];
175	}
176	
177	// Augment GuildConfig so `guild().guildConfig().lattice` is typed without
178	// requiring a manual type parameter at the call site.
179	declare module '@shardworks/nexus-core' {
180	  interface GuildConfig {
181	    lattice?: LatticeConfig;
182	  }
183	}
184	
185	// ── Request / filter types ───────────────────────────────────────────
186	
187	/**
188	 * Request to emit a new pulse. The Lattice fills in `id`, `deliveryState`
189	 * (`'pending'`), and timestamps; the caller supplies everything else.
190	 */
191	export interface EmitPulseRequest {
192	  /** Plugin id of the emitter (stamped onto `pulse.source`). */
193	  source: string;
194	  /** Trigger type — fully-qualified `{pluginId}.{kebab-suffix}` id. */
195	  triggerType: string;
196	  /** Optional writ this pulse is about. Null for non-writ-scoped pulses. */
197	  writId?: string | null;
198	  /** Short human-readable title. */
199	  title: string;
200	  /** Plain-text summary. */
201	  summary: string;
202	  /** Optional deep link (always null in MVP). */
203	  linkUrl?: string | null;
204	  /** Structured payload keyed by trigger type. Defaults to `{}`. */
205	  context?: Record<string, unknown>;
206	}
207	
208	/**
209	 * Filters accepted by `LatticeApi.list` / `LatticeApi.count`.
210	 */
211	export interface PulseFilters {
212	  /** Filter by triggerType. Accepts a single value or an array (OR). */
213	  triggerType?: string | string[];
214	  /** Filter by source plugin id. */
215	  source?: string;
216	  /** Only pulses created at or after this ISO timestamp. */
217	  since?: string;
218	  /** Only pulses created at or before this ISO timestamp. */
219	  until?: string;
220	  /** Filter by delivery state. Accepts a single value or an array (OR). */
221	  deliveryState?: PulseDeliveryState | PulseDeliveryState[];
222	  /** Maximum number of results (default: 20). */
223	  limit?: number;
224	  /** Number of results to skip. */
225	  offset?: number;
226	}
227	
228	// ── API ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
229	
230	/**
231	 * The Lattice's runtime API — retrieved via `guild().apparatus<LatticeApi>('lattice')`.
232	 *
233	 * The surface mirrors `ClerkApi`'s read-side shape: `emit`, `show`, `list`,
234	 * `count`, `resolveId`. There is no `update` or `delete` — pulses are
235	 * immutable event records. The only mutable field on a pulse is
236	 * `deliveryState`, and the Lattice itself is the only writer.
237	 */
238	export interface LatticeApi {
239	  /**
240	   * Emit a pulse. Assigns an id, timestamps, and `deliveryState: 'pending'`,
241	   * writes it to the pulses book, and returns the persisted document.
242	   *
243	   * Dispatch is handled asynchronously by the Lattice's Phase 2 CDC
244	   * watcher — `emit()` does not wait for delivery.
245	   */
246	  emit(request: EmitPulseRequest): Promise<PulseDoc>;
247	
248	  /** Show a pulse by id. Throws if not found. */
249	  show(id: string): Promise<PulseDoc>;
250	
251	  /**
252	   * Resolve a pulse id prefix to the full id. Throws when no pulse matches
253	   * or when the prefix is ambiguous.
254	   */
255	  resolveId(prefix: string): Promise<string>;
256	
257	  /**
258	   * List pulses with optional filters, ordered by createdAt descending.
259	   * Default limit is 20; default `since` is 24h before now.
260	   */
261	  list(filters?: PulseFilters): Promise<PulseDoc[]>;
262	
263	  /** Count pulses matching optional filters. */
264	  count(filters?: PulseFilters): Promise<number>;
265	}
266
